title: Gum on Cotton
stainChemistry: Chewing gum is a hydrophobic polymer (polyisobutylene or chicle) mixed with softeners, sweeteners, and flavorings. On cotton, the polymer adheres through mechanical bonding and surface tension. Warm gum is soft and sticky, spreading when touched. Cold gum becomes brittle and loses adhesion. The polymer is not water-soluble at any temperature.
whyThisWorks: Freezing makes the gum polymer brittle on cotton, breaking its mechanical bond and allowing it to be scraped or cracked off in pieces. POG or citrus-based solvent dissolves any remaining residue by breaking down the polymer. Ice in a bag (not direct ice) prevents water damage while providing the cold needed. Picking at warm gum spreads it — always freeze first.
defaultAssumption: Assume stain age is unknown and possibly heat-set unless user specifies otherwise. Start conservative.

steps: 1. Freeze with CO2 spray or ice to harden gum completely
2. Crack and scrape off hardened gum with dull edge
3. Apply POG to dissolve remaining residue
4. Apply NSD to remove final traces. Flush with cool water
warnings: Always test on hidden area first

homeSolutions.agent: Don't pull at warm gum — freeze it first
homeSolutions.instruction: Warm gum is soft and sticky; pulling or picking at it stretches the gum into the cotton weave and makes the problem 5× worse. First rule: don't touch it yet. Ice the gum until it's rock-hard and brittle. Soft → hard is the whole game — once the gum is frozen solid, it snaps off cleanly.

homeSolutions.agent: Freeze — ice in a bag, not on fabric
homeSolutions.instruction: Place a ziplock bag of ice cubes (or frozen peas) directly on top of the gum. Leave 15–20 minutes. If the item fits, you can put it in the freezer in a ziplock for 30 minutes instead — faster and cleaner. Never apply ice directly to cotton, condensation can bloom a separate water-ring stain.

homeSolutions.agent: Crack and peel — work the frozen gum off
homeSolutions.instruction: Once the gum is hard, flex the fabric to crack the gum. Lift the biggest chunks off with a butter knife or the edge of a credit card. Work from outside in. If any piece stretches or smears, stop — it's warming up. Re-freeze another 10 minutes and try again. Repeat until as much gum is off as possible. Don't panic about residue — that's step 4.

Dab a drop of isopropyl alcohol or Goo Gone on an inside seam and blot with a white cloth after 60 seconds. If the fabric holds its color, you can use solvent in step 5. If dye transfers, skip to step 6 (Dawn only) and expect to escalate to a pro for stubborn residue.

homeSolutions.agent: Solvent attack on residue — Goo Gone, alcohol, or peanut butter oil
homeSolutions.instruction: For residue still stuck to fiber, apply a small amount of one of: Goo Gone (orange/citrus-based dissolver), isopropyl alcohol 91% (dab with cotton ball), OR the old trick — a dab of smooth peanut butter (the oil dissolves the gum polymer). Let it sit 5 minutes. The solvent dissolves the polyisobutylene polymer so it releases from the fiber. Scrape or lift off the dissolved residue with the credit-card edge.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Skip alcohol on silk, wool, rayon, or acetate. Cotton tolerates it well. Goo Gone is safe on most cotton but can leave an oil ring — step 6 handles that.

homeSolutions.agent: Dawn dish soap — clean up the solvent oil
homeSolutions.instruction: Both Goo Gone and peanut butter leave an oil film. Apply a small drop of clear Dawn directly on the spot, work it in dry with a fingertip for 30 seconds, let sit 5 minutes, then blot with a clean damp cloth. Dawn emulsifies the oil so it rinses clean.

Launder on warm (not hot) with your normal detergent. Hang or lay flat, inspect in daylight. Any sticky or oily shadow means residual polymer — repeat step 5 before using the dryer. Dryer heat on residual gum liquefies it and can transfer to other clothes in the load.

homeSolutions.agent: Escalate to a dry cleaner for stubborn residue
homeSolutions.instruction: If the gum survived through a hot dryer (now melted INTO the weave), or residue survives two full home rounds, take it to a dry cleaner. Tell them: "Gum on cotton, [fresh/heat-set]. Bulk removed by freeze + peel; treated residue with [Goo Gone / alcohol] + Dawn. No hot water used at home." Pros have POG and professional-grade citrus solvents that cleave stuck polymer.

steps: 1. Place ice in a bag on the gum for 15 minutes until completely hard
2. Crack the frozen gum and peel/scrape off with a dull edge
3. Apply Dawn dish soap to remaining residue. Work gently
4. Rinse with warm water. Repeat if needed
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if the stain hasn't improved after 3 attempts. Take to a professional for specialized treatment
warnings: Always test cleaning solution on a hidden area first
Air dry until stain is confirmed gone — heat sets most stains

neverDo: No prolonged acid exposure on colored cotton
No chlorine bleach on colored cotton — strips dyes
fiberSensitivities: Test colorfastness on colored cotton before peroxide

escalation.when: If gum stain persists after 2-3 professional treatment cycles, or if the stain has been heat-set, or if home treatment has not improved the stain after 3 attempts
escalation.whatToTell: Explain that the stain is gum on cotton, how old it is, and what products were already used. This helps the professional choose the right chemistry without duplicating failed approaches

contextQuestions: Has it been exposed to heat (dryer, iron, hot water)?
Have you already tried treating it? If so, with what products?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Stain age and heat exposure are the two biggest factors. Fresh stains with no heat exposure have the highest success rate. Heat-set or aged stains may require professional treatment.
